item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… can I use my lifes savings to buy the earth? … cool! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What currency do you use in intergalactic trade?

Starbucks.

Also the price tag is bloody small compared to size of Earth, I won't be surprised if no one approaches to trade.

+1 since it is quite interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beating the Foundation over the head with a single line was (unfortunately) always how I imagined this article. I knew not everyone would like it, but I didn't want to change it from how I first envisioned it 2 years ago. I felt a succinct article was necessary, as one of the more minimal articles of the entire SCP catalogue.

A common theme among all the LCE articles is seemingly impractical methods to accomplish a normal goal. I saw no reason for the salesperson working in 2935 to be all mysterious and cryptic like the previous articles, since the "person" working their day job in it had no reason to talk like that. They're just working an incredibly stupid day job in selling the Earth to "buyers".

Narratively, the article suffers for it. But that's just because not all articles would have a narrative progression in-universe, which is where I think this article stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP, how ever I don't like the second and last part of the back story you wrote. I really think something less mysterious and something that concerns more towards who is buying earth would make a better story

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would have been really good if it didn't beat you over the head with the point at the end of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also do not like the last bit of the interview. The tone was very different from the SCP's previous dismissive replies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler?

+ show block
– hide block

Basically. You had me primed with a juicy concept. A giant price tag on our planet immediately invokes a feeling of insignificance that I love to come across in SCPs. It's something that immediately challenges out place on the world and reminds us how little we matter.

Next, you get into the tag moving. An excellent detail, I'm all for that shit. There's some larger imperceptible, incomprehensible force at work here. I'm at the edge of my seat.

Finally, we get to the shack. It's set up perfectly: no way in, limited knowledge of its interior, and due to its nature, limited information transfer. It's built up as if we're only going to get a peek at whatever's going on…and then we're blatantly told 'yup, that stuff you already knew? Its going on.' End.

It's a wonderful buildup that just fizzles out right at the end, which is disappointing. I'd definitely reconsider my vote should this be retooled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers below

+ show block
– hide block

So you did catch what the final lines of dialogue were implying, but you didn't care for it?

As in, the fact that this giant price tag is being handled constantly, no real evidence there's human buyers, etc.

If this is the case, then I understand you vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was really into this right up until the last bit of the interview. I was hoping to get an added bit of wtf/extra layer of mystery, but it's just directly confirming what I already knew. Hesitant -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at the giant spinning price tag and kept going. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this, although "hecking" makes me think that she is a snek.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to purchase the Earth, but I ain't got the money… Yet…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So many good album releases this year, but 22, A Million is definitely one of the best. Replayed that album about 20+ times by now, literally not one track I dislike on there.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Almost 2 years since I wrote an article.

So here's a short epilogue of some sorts for a certain group I've written up. If I had been in the available for that short story contest I probably would have done this but shorter. Or not I don't know

Picture by Ell Duke, Source here.

Tale to go along with this article: Radiance and Cachinnation
